# Hero Section — Modern Landing Page Redesign

## User Story

**As a** prospective client visiting the Interview Coach website,  
**I want to** see a striking hero section with a clear headline, supporting copy, and prominent calls to action,  
**so that** I can immediately understand the service offering and navigate to what matters most to me.

---

## Description

The landing page currently lacks a strong first impression. This story delivers the redesigned hero section: an asymmetric two-column layout with expressive typography, a diagonal accent rule, animated value chips, and two anchor CTAs. It establishes the visual design tokens (CSS variables for surface, text, accent, spacing, motion) that all subsequent sections depend on.

**Business value:** A compelling hero reduces bounce rate and directs visitors toward services and contact, directly supporting lead conversion.

---

## Scenario

### Scenario 1: First-time visitor lands on the page

**Given** a prospective client navigates to the landing page  
**When** the page finishes loading  
**Then** a full-viewport hero section is displayed with a bold headline using the expressive serif/slab display typeface  
**And** a concise sub-copy paragraph appears below the headline  
**And** two CTAs are visible — a primary button labelled based on site properties and a secondary button — anchored to `#contact` and `#services` respectively  
**And** a set of floating value chips (differentiating metrics or benefit statements) appear on the right column with a staggered reveal animation

### Scenario 2: Page loads with animated reveal

**Given** a visitor's browser does not have `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` set  
**When** the hero section enters the viewport  
**Then** the headline, sub-copy, CTAs, and value chips each animate in with a staggered delay using CSS transitions defined by the motion timing tokens  
**And** the diagonal accent rule decorates the layout without obstructing readable content

### Scenario 3: Visitor has reduced-motion preference

**Given** a visitor has `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` set in their OS or browser  
**When** the page loads  
**Then** all stagger and transition animations are suppressed  
**And** all hero content is fully visible immediately, with no layout shift

### Scenario 4: Visitor clicks the primary CTA

**Given** the hero section is visible  
**When** the visitor clicks the primary CTA button  
**Then** the page scrolls smoothly to the `#contact` section

### Scenario 5: Visitor clicks the secondary CTA

**Given** the hero section is visible  
**When** the visitor clicks the secondary CTA button  
**Then** the page scrolls smoothly to the `#services` section

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Core Functionality
- [ ] Hero section spans the full viewport height (or a defined large fraction) on desktop
- [ ] Headline renders in the expressive serif/slab display font stack
- [ ] Sub-copy renders in the readable sans-serif body font stack
- [ ] Two CTA buttons are present: primary linked to `#contact`, secondary linked to `#services`
- [ ] Right column displays floating value chips with benefit statements
- [ ] A diagonal accent rule is present as a decorative layout element

### Design Tokens
- [ ] CSS custom properties are declared for: surface colours, text colours, accent/muted tones, shadows, border-radius, and motion timing durations
- [ ] Neutral grey base matches the existing app global background colour
- [ ] Accent gradient is applied only to key moments (marker, chips, final CTA) — not globally

### User Experience
- [ ] Staggered CSS reveal animation plays on page load for headline, sub-copy, CTAs, and value chips (in that order)
- [ ] All animations respect the `prefers-reduced-motion` media query and are disabled when set
- [ ] CTA buttons provide visible focus indicators for keyboard navigation
- [ ] Both CTAs scroll to the target section without a full page reload

### Responsive Layout
- [ ] On desktop (≥1024px): two-column asymmetric layout is displayed
- [ ] On tablet (768px–1023px): layout adapts gracefully; value chips remain visible
- [ ] On mobile (<768px): single-column layout; CTAs stack vertically; no horizontal overflow

### Accessibility
- [ ] Hero heading uses a single `<h1>` tag
- [ ] Value chips and decorative elements do not break screen reader flow (use `aria-hidden` where appropriate)
- [ ] Colour contrast of text on hero background meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for body, 3:1 for large text)

---

## Prerequisites

_No prerequisite user stories — this is the foundational story that introduces the design token system._
